assignment_description:
Reading the Bible for a Change
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
If I were to ask how you approach reading the Bible, what would you say? Do you have a color-coded system, dozens of notes written in the margins, and your name proudly stamped in the front of your Bible? Or are the pages of your Bible untouched? Perhaps you write everything down in a notebook? Or perhaps the Bible sits on your shelf and remains untouched. Whatever your answer, I invite you to put aside your pre-conceived notions about Bible reading and listen as Ray Lubeck invites you to a new way of reading the Bible.

➜ ANNOTATE
Reading the Bible for a Change is a non-fiction book, which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text, you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students may record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ
Prologue p.1-5

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ WRITE
On page 3, Ray lists a couple of questions that have stayed with him for many years. Choose two of the questions and try to answer them in two paragraphs. You may want to refer back to these questions and your answers from time to time.

➜ PONDER
In the prologue of Reading the Bible for a Change, Ray Lubeck tells the story of how someone misused the Bible when he was very little. He also tells the reader about the effects that this misuse had upon him. Think back to a time when the Bible was misused either by you or someone around you. Did you ever confront the person? How should we handle these misuses of the Bible?

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 1, What is the Bible

Reading as Following
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Why read the Bible? What does it tell us? What are we supposed to take away from our reading? These are just some of the questions that Ray approaches in the first chapter. Think on these questions and don't be afraid to write down any that come to mind as you read.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.1 p.9-17

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ WRITE
Ray Lubeck lists three hermeneutical virtues on pages 14-15. In one paragraph, describe how you see these virtues in your own reading of the Bible.

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 2, The Story of the Bible.

The Bible and Literary Categories
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
In this chapter, Ray Lubeck writes, "How we read any piece of literature depends completely on what kind of literature it is that we think we are reading." Think about how this impacts your reading of the Bible as you read through the chapter.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.2 p.18-25 stop at "a particular passage."

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 3, Literary Styles

⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ RECAP
Think back to last week's reading as you continue in the chapter.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.2 p.25-31 "Genres of Biblical" through the end of the chapter.

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS

Introduction to the Four Steps
⍞ Materials: A Bible Study with Four Steps

➜ INTRO
Ray Lubeck writes a four-step Bible study method which includes the following: seeing, understanding, connecting, and responding.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.3 p.35-43 end at "others have missed."

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

Introduction to the Four Steps continued...
⍞ Materials: A Bible Study with Four Steps

➜ RECAP
Think back to last week's reading.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.3 p.43-49 start at "3.3 How does this text relate to its context?"

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ WRITE
After you have read through the section, write two paragraphs. The first paragraph will be about which of the four steps you are most familiar with or comfortable with. The second paragraph will be about which of the steps you are unfamiliar with or uncomfortable with.

The First Step: Seeing
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Ray Lubeck opens up chapter four by saying, "The first step to take in following the Bible is learning to see it carefully. Of course, I realize that anyone who has read the Bible has already 'seen' it—the words on the page are seen. But I have more in mind than this." What do you think he has in mind? What do you think "seeing" means when it comes to reading the Bible?

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.4 p.50-56

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ PONDER
In what ways did this chapter challenge the way you read the Bible? What was a new idea, and what has been heard before?

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 5, Plot
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 6, Character
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 7 Setting

The Second Step: Understanding
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Have you ever read something in the Bible and thought, "What does it mean?" If you have, then you are already beginning to look for the second step: understanding.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.5 p.57-65 end at "as presented in the text."

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ PONDER
On page 63, Ray Lubeck writes, "Let me put it very bluntly: A Bible reader who is more concerned with his or her own 'meaning' in a passage is a very selfish person." Think about this sentence. Do you agree or disagree with it? Why?

The Second Step: Understanding continued...
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ RECAP
Think back to last week's reading on understanding.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.5 p.65-79 start at "Hermeneutical Foundations for Bible Study"

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ WRITE
On 64-73, Ray Lubeck lists 12 hermeneutical foundations for Bible study. Write one paragraph about the foundations that are most familiar or comfortable to you. Write one paragraph about the foundations that are least familiar or uncomfortable to you.

The Third Step: Connecting
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Ray Lubeck writes, "A wise person knows how to see patterns and relationships and is able to explain why they matter." As you read this section, I invite you to think about this statement.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.6 p.80-93 end on "Old Testament ethics."

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

The Third Step: Connecting continued...
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ RECAP
Think back to last week's reading. What do you remember? What stood out to you?

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.6 p.93-105 From "Connecting Paul and Moses" - "intentional voice."

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

Reading the Bible for a Change

The Third Step: Connecting continued...
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ RECAP
Think back to last week's reading. What do you remember? What stood out to you?

➜ READ
Ch.6 p.105-116 "Following the Bible" through the end of the chapter.

➜ ANNOTATE
Reading the Bible for a Change is a non-fiction book, which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text, you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students may record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ WRITE
At the end of this chapter, Ray Lubeck writes, "As you can see, life itself is a story to be lived. The Bible is not intended to be part of my story, but the other way around—my story is subsumed by the world-story that the Bible presents. Life makes sense as I align myself with the one meta-story. When I receive it as a comprehensive story, I understand the parts more clearly: both the parts of the Bible and all the parts of my life." Respond to the quote in 1-2 paragraphs. Do you agree or disagree? Why or why not? How does this statement inform your reading of the Bible?

➜ PONDER
What are the ideas from this section that you are taking away? What are you applying to your own Bible reading?

The Fourth Step: Responding
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Have you ever had the experience of reading the Bible and then asking, "So what?" If you have, you're not alone. And the fourth step, responding, will help you answer this question. As always, remember to take note of any questions that your reading brings up. You can make a physical note of these to make it easier to remember.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.7 p.117-127 end at "into personal autobiography."

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ WRITE
Ray Lubeck writes, "In the courses that I teach on Bible study, I have given assignments that required the students to list specific response suggestions that relate the shared Truth claims they have discovered in the text to their own lives. In observing their tendencies, I have been struck by two things: most of their suggestions have to do with outward actions rather than attitudes and perspectives, and they lack this element of imagination." Respond to this statement in one paragraph.

The Fourth Step: Responding continued...
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ RECAP
Think back to last week's reading. What do you remember? What stood out to you?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.7 p.127-136 "Responding as the" through the end of the chapter.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ PONDER
Ray Lubeck writes, "It is through Responding that we translate our theology into personal autobiography." How does this statement resonate with you?

How to Read Narrative
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Are you a fan of narratives? Perhaps you like reading stories like The Lord of the Rings or Narnia? Or maybe you like to read biographical material about the lives of people you respect or who stir your curiosity. How about movies like Star Wars? Did you know that your brain is hardwired for these narratives? The Bible is overflowing with the genre of narrative, but that means we need to read it a certain way. As you spend time in this next chapter, take note of ideas that stand out to you.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.8 p.139-150 stop at "the entire story."

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS

Reading Narrative: The Book of Ruth
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
In last week's reading, Ray Lubeck wrote, "Every narrative has a setting, the environment in which the action takes place. It also includes characters, who are the participants in the actions. And it develops a plot, a series of events that are linked together. In addition to these three, all narratives employ elements of style or technique. Whenever we are reading a biblical narrative, we should look for how these elements are presented by the author." As you read through this next chapter, think about how each of these elements is presented by the author.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.12 p.215-235

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

How to Read Poetry
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Poetry is also a genre we find in the Bible. What is your familiarity with poems? Do you have any favorites? How might reading Biblical poetry be different than reading other types of poetry? Think about these questions as you read through the section. Additionally, if you have any questions, I encourage you to write them down so you can refer back to them.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.9 p.164-179

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 9, Poetry
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 10, Poetic Metaphor

Reading Poetry: 1 Samuel 2:1-10
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ RECAP
Think back to last week's reading. What do you remember? What stood out to you?

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.13 p.235-247

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

How to Read Discourse
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Ray Lubeck writes, "Yet when it comes to the most important written documents of culture, most often these come in the form of discourse." Think about popular discourses that you've read that are not on the list that he provides on pages 180-181. How have these discourses impacted your life and the lives of others? Additionally, if you have any questions, I encourage you to write them down so you can refer back to them.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch. 10 p.180-192

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ WRITE
Write two paragraphs on how you can apply what you've learned to your Bible reading.

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 17, New Testament Letters: Historical Context
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 18, New Testament Letters: Literary Context

Reading Discourse: The Book of Philippians
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
So how does one read discourse? Ray Lubeck uses the book of Philippians to show his readers an example of how to approach discourse. Additionally, if you have any questions, I encourage you to write them down so you can refer back to them.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.14 p.245-269

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ PONDER
Write two paragraphs dedicated to answering the following questions: How have you read discourse in the past? What do you intend to apply to your reading of discourse in the future?

Biblical Genres
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
You've learned about Narrative, Poetry, and Discourse, which are the predominant genres in the Scriptures. Now, Ray Lubeck turns to other biblical genres and how to read through them. From Apocalyptic, epistle, and gospel, to prophecy, proverbial, and Psalm, you'll learn how to navigate the other genres. Additionally, if you have any questions, I encourage you to write them down so you can refer back to them.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.11 p.193-212

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ PONDER
Which of the genres listed in the chapter are you most familiar with? Do you tend to gravitate towards one genre or another? Why or why not?

➜ EXTRA HELPINGS

∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 12, The Prophets
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 13, Biblical Law
∞ Video Link: Bible Project, Episode 15, The Gospel

⍞ Materials: God of All Things

➜ INTRO
This is a book all about things. But the things can point us to God. So, really, this is a book all about things and God and how we can be reminded of him when we see and interact with the things of this world. There are new wonders to see all around us!

➜ ANNOTATE
Reading the Bible for a Change is a non-fiction book, which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text, you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students may record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Foreword, Introduction, Ch.1-2
Maybe a chart, diagram, or drawing for some of the chapters would be a good way to narrate. Or, maybe writing a poem or a song about each item might make sense to capture the truths of the chapter. Try a haiku with 5,7, and 5 syllables for each line. See what works well for you.
